model_input:
Question: A 67-year-old man with dyslipidemia, obesity (body mass index, 34), past tobacco use, anxiety, and borderline hypertension presented to the emergency department (ED) after 3 days of shortness of breath on exertion. He described increased anxiety and a pleuritic chest pain that began a few hours prior to his ED visit, but no hemoptysis. He had no personal history of cancer, venous thrombosis, recent surgery, trauma, or admission to hospital. His medications included fluoxetine, atorvastatin, vitamin D, magnesium, and niacin.On examination, his blood pressure was 164/82 mm Hg; pulse rate, 104/min; and respiratory rate, 18/min. Oxygen saturation level was 96% in room air. His lungs were clear to auscultation bilaterally. No extra heart sounds or murmurs were noted. Examination of his extremities were unremarkable with no leg symptoms or swelling or pain at calf palpation. Laboratory test results showed a hemoglobin level of 160 g/dL (reference range, 14.0-17.5 g/dL), a platelet count of 129 103/ L (reference range, 150-350 103/ L), and a serum creatinine level of 1.26 mg/dL (reference range, 0.6-1.2 mg.dL) (111  mol/L; reference range, 13-106  mol/L). His chest film was normal. The attending physician s presumptive diagnosis was pulmonary embolism (PE). His pretest clinical probability of PE according to the 2-level Wells score1 was likely (1.5 points for heart rate >100, and 3.0 for other diagnoses less likely than PE). Therefore, D-dimer measurement was not performed. His computed tomographic pulmonary angiography (CTPA) result is reported in the Table.Rule out PE and perform a work up for alternative diagnoses.Obtain a bilateral lower limb veins compression ultrasonography.Obtain a lung ventilation-perfusion (V /Q ) scan or pulmonary angiogram. What Would You Do Next?
Options: A:Rule out PE and perform a work up for alternative diagnoses. B:Obtain a bilateral lower limb veins compression ultrasonography. C:Obtain a lung ventilation-perfusion (V /Q ) scan or pulmonary angiogram. D:Test for D-dimer.
 Which option is the correct answer to the question? ALWAYS provide the option index of the final answer AT THE END with TRIPLE BACKTICKS among A, B, C, D, EXACTLY like this: The answer is ```X```. DO NOT add any other text or variations.
opa: Rule out PE and perform a work up for alternative diagnoses.
opb: Obtain a bilateral lower limb veins compression ultrasonography.
opc: Obtain a lung ventilation-perfusion (V /Q ) scan or pulmonary angiogram.
answer: Rule out PE and perform a work up for alternative diagnoses.
explanation: CTPA is the most-often used imaging diagnostic test for PE. In contrast with the V /Q  scan, it allows direct visualization of pulmonary arterial circulation after intravenous injection of an iodinated contrast agent. CTPA should be used in combination with a pretest clinical probability assessment.1 Accepted diagnostic criteria for PE on CTPA are (1) lack of opacification of the vascular lumen due to a central filling defect; (2) a partial filling defect surrounded by contrast material on a cross-sectional image or contrast material between the central filling defect and the artery wall on a longitudinal image; or (3) a peripheral intraluminal defect that forms an acute angle with the artery.2Multidetector CTPA showed excellent accuracy for PE (sensitivity 100%, specificity 89%) when compared with pulmonary angiography (criterion standard for PE) in an accuracy study.3 A negative CTPA has been shown to safely rule out the diagnosis of PE as demonstrated in several studies in which there was a very low 3-month risk (1.2%, [95% CI, 0.8%-1.8%]) of venous thromboembolism in patients left untreated after a negative CTPA, similar to the risk observed after a negative pulmonary angiography.4CTPA results should be reported using standardized information to facilitate evaluation of the test s quality. Information should include overall examination quality, adequacy of pulmonary arterial enhancement, anatomic level down to which PE can confidently be excluded, and presence or absence of artifacts or other factors affecting scan quality.5 In the case of PE, the location and most proximal level of the thrombus should be reported. The Medicare national price for CTPA, without contrast material followed by contrast material (Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System [HCPCS] 71275) is $300.70.Although CTPA was indicated in this case, in patients who are considered as unlikely to have PE (Wells model PE probability <13%), a D-dimer test should be performed. Combined with an unlikely pretest clinical probability, a negative D-dimer test allows the clinician to rule out PE without an imaging test in approximately one-third of nonhospitalized patients (often ED patients) with suspected PE.6His CTPA is negative for PE. No imaging for PE is required for this patient. In particular, a meta-analysis demonstrated that that it is not necessary to combine a negative compression ultrasonography with a negative multidetector CTPA to rule out PE.7 Consider PE ruled out (answer A) and assess for alternative diagnoses.The other option in this patient would have been to perform a V /Q  scan. A frequent criticism to this test is the high proportion of inconclusive test results. However, diagnostic algorithms based on the V /Q  have proven as safe and effective as CTPA.8 V /Q  scan has some advantages over CTPA: no contraindication in case of renal failure, no risk from contrast dye, and lower radiation exposure. A V /Q  scan might answer the increasing concern about overdiagnosis of PE with CTPA. A randomized controlled trial of 2 management strategies for suspected PE (CTPA vs V /Q  scan) demonstrated a 5% increase (19% vs 14%) in the proportion of confirmed PEs with CTPA, but no difference in the 3-month thromboembolic risk after PE was ruled out.8 A US time trend analysis revealed an increase in the incidence of PE after the introduction of CTPA, with minimal change in mortality and a lower case fatality, consistent with overdiagnosis.9 CTPA is associated with an increase in the proportion of PEs limited to subsegmental arteries, which is of debatable clinical relevance.10The patient was discharged with no medication changes after an electrocardiogram and assessment of his troponin levels. Diagnosis was increased anxiety with uneventful 6-month follow-up.CTPA should be used in patients with suspected PE and a likely pretest probability or positive D-Dimer test.A negative CTPA safely rules out PE and might provide an alternative diagnosis.Diagnostic algorithms based on V /Q  scan are a valid alternative.The clinical relevance and management of subsegmental PE diagnosed on CTPA remains debated.

He described increased anxiety and a pleuritic chest pain that began a few hours prior to his ED visit, but no hemoptysis. His medications included fluoxetine, atorvastatin, vitamin D, magnesium, and niacin.On examination, his blood pressure was 164/82 mm Hg; pulse rate, 104/min; and respiratory rate, 18/min. Oxygen saturation level was 96% in room air. His lungs were clear to auscultation bilaterally. No extra heart sounds or murmurs were noted. Examination of his extremities were unremarkable with no leg symptoms or swelling or pain at calf palpation. Laboratory test results showed a hemoglobin level of 160 g/dL (reference range, 14.0-17.5 g/dL), a platelet count of 129 103/ L (reference range, 150-350 103/ L), and a serum creatinine level of 1.26 mg/dL (reference range, 0.6-1.2 mg.dL) (111  mol/L; reference range, 13-106  mol/L). His pretest clinical probability of PE according to the 2-level Wells score1 was likely (1.5 points for heart rate >100, and 3.0 for other diagnoses less likely than PE). Therefore, D-dimer measurement was not performed. His computed tomographic pulmonary angiography (CTPA) result is reported in the Table.Rule out PE and perform a work up for alternative diagnoses.Obtain a bilateral lower limb veins compression ultrasonography.Obtain a lung ventilation-perfusion (V /Q ) scan or pulmonary angiogram. What Would You Do Next?

The correct answer is A: Rule out PE and perform a work up for alternative diagnoses.

The attending physician has already diagnosed pulmonary embolism (PE) based on clinical suspicion and the Wells score. Since PE has been ruled out, the next logical step is to work up for alternative causes of the patient's symptoms. This includes imaging studies to differentiate between PE and other conditions such as pulmonary fibrosis or interstitial lung disease. Therefore, performing a bilateral lower limb compression ultrasonography, a lung ventilation-perfusion scan, or a pulmonary angiogram would be appropriate next steps.
